name: Thoreau & Walden
description: Simplicity, solitude, and deliberate living from the sage of Walden Pond.
author: Quips Editorial

quotes.content: I went to the woods because I wished to live deliberately, to front only the essential facts of life, and see if I could not learn what it had to teach, and not, when I came to die, discover that I had not lived.
quotes.source: Walden, "Where I Lived, and What I Lived For" (1854)
quotes.notes: The book's most quoted passage, stating the purpose of Thoreau's two-year experiment at the pond.

quotes.content: I wanted to live deep and suck out all the marrow of life, to live so sturdily and Spartan-like as to put to rout all that was not life.
quotes.source: Walden, "Where I Lived, and What I Lived For" (1854)
quotes.notes: Continues the 'live deliberately' passage with Thoreau's appetite for essential experience.

quotes.content: Our life is frittered away by detail... Simplify, simplify.
quotes.source: Walden, "Where I Lived, and What I Lived For" (1854)
quotes.notes: Ellipsis marks omitted text; the twinned 'Simplify, simplify' follows the same paragraph as Thoreau's call for a spare life.

quotes.content: The mass of men lead lives of quiet desperation.
quotes.notes: Perhaps the most famous line of the book, from the opening chapter's critique of conventional labor.

quotes.content: As if you could kill time without injuring eternity.
quotes.notes: Thoreau's rebuke to those who treat time as something merely to be spent or wasted.

quotes.content: The cost of a thing is the amount of what I will call life which is required to be exchanged for it, immediately or in the long run.
quotes.notes: Frequently mangled as 'the price of anything is the amount of life you exchange for it'; this is Thoreau's actual wording.

quotes.content: Public opinion is a weak tyrant compared with our own private opinion.
quotes.notes: On self-reliance: the harshest judge of a person is their own inner conscience, not the crowd.

quotes.content: A man is rich in proportion to the number of things which he can afford to let alone.
quotes.source: Walden, "Where I Lived, and What I Lived For" (1854)
quotes.notes: Thoreau redefines wealth as freedom from possessions rather than their accumulation.

quotes.content: Time is but the stream I go a-fishing in.
quotes.source: Walden, "Where I Lived, and What I Lived For" (1854)
quotes.notes: A meditation on drinking from the current of time while glimpsing eternity beneath it.

quotes.content: I never found the companion that was so companionable as solitude.
quotes.notes: From the chapter defending the richness of a life lived largely alone at the pond.

quotes.content: I had three chairs in my house; one for solitude, two for friendship, three for society.
quotes.notes: Thoreau's playful accounting of hospitality in his one-room cabin.

quotes.content: Not till we are lost, in other words, not till we have lost the world, do we begin to find ourselves.
quotes.notes: On the disorienting walk home in the dark as a metaphor for self-discovery.

quotes.content: Every man is the builder of a temple, called his body.
quotes.notes: From the chapter on discipline, purity, and the reverence owed to one's own life.

quotes.content: Heaven is under our feet as well as over our heads.
quotes.notes: Written while sounding the frozen depths of Walden Pond, finding the divine in the ordinary earth.

quotes.content: We need the tonic of wildness.
quotes.notes: Often misprinted as 'tonic of wilderness'; Thoreau's word is 'wildness.'

quotes.content: We can never have enough of nature.
quotes.notes: From the same passage praising wildness as a restorative for the human spirit.

quotes.content: However mean your life is, meet it and live it; do not shun it and call it hard names.
quotes.notes: Closing counsel to embrace one's actual life rather than envy another's.

quotes.content: If one advances confidently in the direction of his dreams, and endeavors to live the life which he has imagined, he will meet with a success unexpected in common hours.
quotes.notes: The genuine source of the popular but loose paraphrase 'Go confidently in the direction of your dreams.'

quotes.content: Rather than love, than money, than fame, give me truth.
quotes.notes: Thoreau's ranking of truth above the ordinary objects of ambition.

quotes.content: Things do not change; we change.
quotes.notes: On the inward locus of transformation in the book's final chapter.

quotes.content: I wish to speak a word for Nature, for absolute freedom and wildness, as contrasted with a freedom and culture merely civil.
quotes.notes: The opening sentence of the essay, first delivered as a lecture in 1851 and published in The Atlantic in 1862.

quotes.content: The West of which I speak is but another name for the Wild; and what I have been preparing to say is, that in Wildness is the preservation of the World.
quotes.notes: The full line behind the widely misquoted 'in wilderness is the preservation of the world'; Thoreau wrote 'Wildness' and 'preservation.'

quotes.content: Life consists with wildness. The most alive is the wildest.
quotes.notes: Thoreau equates vitality itself with the untamed and unsubdued.

quotes.content: That government is best which governs least.
quotes.notes: The motto Thoreau 'heartily accepts' to open the essay, though he pushes it toward 'governs not at all.'

quotes.content: Under a government which imprisons any unjustly, the true place for a just man is also a prison.
quotes.notes: Written after Thoreau's night in jail for refusing a tax that funded slavery and the Mexican-American War.

quotes.content: Any man more right than his neighbors constitutes a majority of one.
quotes.notes: On the moral authority of the lone individual against the majority.

quotes.content: It is not desirable to cultivate a respect for the law, so much as for the right.
quotes.notes: Thoreau places conscience above statute as the true guide to conduct.

quotes.content: The only obligation which I have a right to assume is to do at any time what I think right.
quotes.notes: A cornerstone of Thoreau's argument for individual conscience over collective demand.

quotes.content: It is not enough to be industrious; so are the ants. What are you industrious about?
quotes.source: Letter to H. G. O. Blake, November 16, 1857
quotes.notes: The genuine original of the popular misquote 'It is not enough to be busy; so are the ants,' from Thoreau's correspondence with Harrison Blake.

quotes.content: Live in each season as it passes; breathe the air, drink the drink, taste the fruit, and resign yourself to the influences of each.
quotes.notes: From Thoreau's private Journal, a distillation of his practice of living attentively in the present.
